originalBook: Of the punishment and results of man’s first sin, and of the propagation of man without lust.
title: Of the Pride in the Sin, Which Was Worse Than the Sin Itself.

source.title: NPNF1-02. St. Augustine's City of God and Christian Doctrine

verses.text: But it is a worse and more damnable pride which casts about for the shelter of an excuse even in manifest sins, as these our first parents did, of whom the woman said, “The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat;” and the man said, “The woman whom Thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat.”
Here there is no word of begging pardon, no word of entreaty for healing.
For though they do not, like Cain, deny that they have perpetrated the deed, yet their pride seeks to refer its wickedness to another,—the woman’s pride to the serpent, the man’s to the woman.
But where there is a plain trangression of a divine commandment, this is rather to accuse than to excuse oneself.

For the fact that the woman sinned on the serpent’s persuasion, and the man at the woman’s offer, did not make the transgression less, as if there were any one whom we ought rather to believe or yield to than God.
